name: Pratul Singh Raghava
title: Software Engineer
tagline: Loves ABCD - Anything Backend Cloud & Data

rating.source: Based on LinkedIn endorsements & peer reviews

bio.short: Software Engineer (First Class Honours, USYD) who builds production-grade systems end to end, from AI pipelines and cloud infrastructure to App Store deployment.
bio.full:
I am a Software Engineer (First Class Honours, USYD) who builds production-grade systems end to end. I lead engineering for Done Life, an AI-first productivity platform, owning the full lifecycle from cloud infrastructure and AI pipelines through to App Store compliance and CI/CD.

My technical foundation spans field-level AES-256-GCM encryption, Terraform-provisioned GCP infrastructure, OpenAI Responses API with strict JSON schema and timezone-aware extraction, and GitHub Actions CI/CD pipelines. At Accenture I acted as a force multiplier, automating security provisioning workflows and cutting manual effort by 80%. Beyond code, I led the USYD Robotics Club from 230 to 1,800+ members as President, and I excel at translating ambiguous requirements into shipped software across compliance, data modelling, and product roadmaps.

coreThemes.title: Build-Run-Own Mindset
coreThemes.description: I don't just write code; I architect the solution, manage the cloud operations, and handle the compliance (from GDPR to App Store verification).

coreThemes.title: AI & Scalability
coreThemes.description: I build systems that handle complexity—from scaling database permissions at Accenture to designing 'Split-Path' AI engines that optimize cost and latency.

coreThemes.title: Clarity
coreThemes.description: I excel at translating technical trade-offs for non-technical stakeholders (clients, students, leadership).

trivia.fact: Can name every country in the world in under 10 minutes
Has FlightRadar24 permanently open for planespotting
Plays chess (rating is... a work in progress)
Scaled USYD Robotics Club from 230 to 1,800+ members
Once accidentally brought down a database at Accenture, then turned it into a security win
Managed a crisis when 1,100 people signed up for a 60-person event
